channel.title: Itay's World
channel.description: Crazy stories and guides from all corners of the planet

item.title: Why wanzaimian (重庆小面) is my favorite food in China
item.description:
Why wanzaimian (重庆小面) is my favorite food in China

I really love those noodles! If I had the option, I would eat them every day of the week. Unfortunately here in Singapore I haven’t found any really good xiaomian yet. The best ones I had were in Chongqing (of course) and in Shenzhen.

Why do I love it so much?

Us laowai hate eating stuff with bones, really. It took me so long to get used to eating meat with bones inside it. In Western culture it’s really not the norm.

Second, we don’t really like wet noodles. I always feel like when we eat noodles with broth, all the flavor stays in the soup and nothing sticks to the noodles.

Here wanzaimian comes to save the day! You have minced meat so you don’t need to worry about bones, and the mala sauce together with the noodle structure makes everything stick together into one perfect bite!

I remember the first time I tried it. I saw the texture of the beans you put inside and it really reminded me of one of my favorite dishes back in my home country - hummus! This really made me fall in love with wanzaimian.

The picture I posted is my only picture with wanzaimian (because I always finish it so fast). It’s me enjoying the noodles in the wild streets of Chongqing.

Do you like it? Should I try any other dishes in China? Send me pictures below of the wanzaimian you guys had and I will judge it!

item.title: Things I Dislike About Hk
item.description:
why I dislike about Hong Kong life

I used to live in Hong Kong for about a year. In the end I decided to leave (you can read the full reasons in my other post).

I have to be honest, I loved Hong Kong, but disliked it at the same time.

Living in Hong Kong, you feel like you are fighting for space with everyone. Walking in the narrow streets can be challenging sometimes, especially during rush hour.

Eating in a restaurant is weird as well, especially coming from a very warm country. I used to small talk with every shopkeeper and waitress, but now it feels like everything is rushed. People want me to just finish eating as fast as possible and leave.

The weirdest thing for me in Hong Kong was people saying “I hate my job”. I don’t think I’m ignorant, but this saying is so weird to me. Why do you want to spend so much time in a job you hate? How can you wake up every day to a job you don’t like that gives you so much stress? Back home, a job is just a job. Sometimes there are better days, sometimes more stressful days, but none of the people I know ever told me they hate their job. Never.

If someone in my country hates their job, they just quit. I feel like we value our time much more than the people who work in Hong Kong - this was really hard for me to accept, and still is.

I’m really asking: Why do you want to spend 9-5 or even 9-9 every day in a place you hate?

I feel like people really need to embrace life, talk to the strangers around us, because we are all here on the same planet, on the same earth. No one really knows what he is doing, so we might as well just enjoy the ride! Be nice to strangers, and don’t let any boss tell you how to live your life.

For me it was too hard to accept this lifestyle. I traveled the world for 1 year before coming here, so it was such a shock. I knew I wouldn’t last long in this city, but I didn’t expect to leave this early.

When I say I also loved Hong Kong, I really mean it. I want to write a similar post in the future talking about the things I loved, so you will not only see my one-sided perspective.

Now I’m living in Singapore. It also has its challenges, but I feel like the pace is way slower. You can walk outside, look at the otters swimming in the river, and not just rush to work or home.

What do you think about this lifestyle? How can you handle it so well? Maybe I’m just not Hong Kong material.

item.title: Climbing Emeishan
item.description:
Part 1

I climbed Emeishan in one day. That was the hardest thing I ever did in my life. I was frustrated, my mental state was not the best. I wanted to quit so many times throughout the hike, but in the end I did it! And I proved to myself that I can do anything!

When I was traveling in Sichuan, I saw that there is this famous hike to Emeishan. I had a couple days to travel alone before meeting my girlfriend, so I decided to do it.

I am a fairly experienced hiker, so I thought it wouldn’t be a problem. I bought a train ticket to Emeishan for the next few days and started preparing. Little did I know I would almost want to quit the hike even before starting it.

The next day me and my girlfriend had a huge fight. We were long-distance and were not next to each other, so it was really hard to put this fight behind us. I kept thinking about it all day - thinking about what I was going to say and how I was going to respond to her when we met. It made me feel drained, and like I was a loser and couldn’t achieve anything.

I really need to work on that emotion in me - but not today. Maybe one day.

I started to become scared of climbing Emeishan. I read online that there are snowstorms sometimes at the top, and there are monkeys that steal your stuff. I was in a bad mindset, and I didn’t have my girlfriend or any friend to tell me “You can do it, you got this!”

Suddenly this challenge, that a few days ago I was like “It’s the easiest challenge ever”, became this huge thing that I needed to overcome without love and support from my loved ones. I felt alone. I just wanted to quit, be sick, or come up with some excuse not to do it.

Just a heads up - I kept talking to my girlfriend all the time, but because we didn’t put this fight behind us, I couldn’t come to peace with it and I felt like she was not really supporting me 100%.

I took the train and kept battling with myself, thinking “I’m not feeling well, maybe I should rest tomorrow”, and when I arrived in the town, I realized I was not prepared at all!! My hiking gear was: running shoes from Taobao, a leather jacket, and my work bag.

This really didn’t help the situation of me feeling lost and unsupported. Like it’s not enough, when I went to the store to buy food and a walking stick, the auntie showed me a photo from the peak taken yesterday. It was covered in snow and you couldn’t even see the pathway.

When I told her I was going to climb the mountain tomorrow, she said it was too dangerous and called me crazy!

I was devastated. I wanted to quit so badly. I decided I was quitting, so I called my girlfriend to tell her the news, and thank god I called her, because she said: “I know I’m mad at you right now, but I support you. I know you can do it. Don’t listen to the auntie, believe in yourself. I believe in you!”

I told her goodnight, packed my bag, turned off the light, and went to sleep, preparing to wake up at 5am to start the hike tomorrow!

Part 2

item.title: Why you should go to GuangZhou if you are foodie
item.description:
Why you should go to GuangZhou if you are a foodie

When we were living in Hong Kong, we went on a weekend getaway to GuangZhou, and to be honest I kind of regret not doing it more often.

If you are a foodie, this place is like heaven on earth. I have been to many cities and provinces in China, but this city has the best food by far. And the funniest thing is we discovered a hidden-gem by accident.

We had a free weekend and didn’t really know where to go. We already traveled to ShenZhen quite often, so we decided to explore a new city. My girlfriend really likes coconut hotpot, and she heard there are very good ones in GuangZhou. Even though I don’t like it that much, I still decided to tell her “yes let’s do it” and thank god I said it, because we discovered one street with some of the best food in China.

We took a train from Hong Kong train station to GuangZhou (only 40 minutes!!!)

After we arrived in the city, we decided to take a taxi to the hotpot place. On the way, suddenly the cab slowed down and we stopped moving :( We looked outside and the street was packed with people and food stalls. Everyone was sitting on the sidewalks and eating so many different kinds of food.

We were so hungry because we didn’t eat the entire day. So we just asked the taxi driver to leave us there, and little did we know we discovered the BEST street food street!

We walked around and entered so many different stores and shops, and tried so many kinds of food.

Food we tried:

  • fried pigeon( 炸乳鸽) - the best pigeon I had in my life
  • black chicken soup (乌鸡药材汤)
  • coconut chicken soup (椰子鸡汤)- inside the coconut itself!!
  • black sesame fudge (芝麻糊)
  • premium durian
  • and much more that I already forgot

The food was so good, so instead of going for a day trip we decided to stay over in GuangZhou. That way we had a chance to fill our belly with more amazing food we hadn’t managed to eat that day.

I posted pictures here of the food. I’m sorry if the quality is not the best. The food was too good so we finished everything before having a chance to take a picture. (I left a picture of me playing ping pong with locals as an apology.)

And I know you might think “Why does this laowai know about Chinese food?” Let me tell you this: I traveled to China 14 times in the past year, all the way from HaiNan to Beijing, so if I see good food I know to stop and appreciate it. If you still don’t believe me, take a look at the comments below.

Secret street location: 司马路/西华路路口

If you have no plans this coming weekend, you must go there and give it a try! I really encourage you to go tomorrow (or on your next day off) and let me know how it goes. I hope I convinced you. Any other places you want to hear my opinion about? Do you think there are places with better food? (please don’t say Beijing - I didn’t really like the food there - but that is another story already)

item.title: Why I left Hong Kong
item.description:
Why I left Hong Kong:

I lived for about 1 year in Hong Kong. This was an amazing yet very chaotic experience. I met my girlfriend there while traveling and decided to stay with her (she lived there for about 3 years).

Hong Kong was really too much for us.

Don’t get me wrong. I think Hong Kong is an amazing travel destination and you should definitely go there. The scenery is amazing and the food is so good.

It’s just not a place to live and build a life in.

Hong Kong people - at least most of them - are rude, not really nice, and too self-centered. Every time I would walk on a crowded street or step into a crowded metro, I felt like I was fighting with everyone for space and to be seen.

Our space in Hong Kong was tiny! (less than 40 sqm). It is really hard to live life in this small “shoe box”.

After some time, I felt Hong Kong got the best of me and made me become a person I don’t want to be. I started to be less nice to people around me, less patient, and got stressed about small things easily. It was really too hard, so we decided to leave.

We just moved to Singapore and so far the experience is very different. The pathways are wide enough so you don’t need to push anyone (hahahah). Our house is much bigger and nicer, there is a lot of greenery around us, and we are just around the corner from a river and a park, so we don’t need to go on a hike to escape the city (like HK). Most importantly, we feel like the work-life balance, while not as good as Europe, is much better and healthier than Hong Kong.

I posted pictures here of us having fun in Hong Kong. I know I spread some hate about Hong Kong here, but we really had some great times and memories there. This place will always have a warm place in my heart. Hopefully one day I can come back as a tourist, go to the horse racing, and experience the big city life.

What is your experience with Hong Kong? I want to know if people have a similar experience to what we had. And what do you think about Singapore? I would love to hear from people who have already lived here for a long time and how it compares to Hong Kong.

What do you prefer?

  • Hong Kong
  • Singapore
  • neither…

item.title: Why you have to go see horse racing while you are in Hong Kong
item.description:
Why you have to go see horse racing while you are in Hong Kong

When I was living in Hong Kong, I had a chance to experience horse racing in Happy Valley, and to be honest, that was one of my best memories from the city.

Before I start, don’t be like “Yeah I live in Hong Kong, I will go check it out one day…” We both know you won’t. Read this and hopefully it will convince you.

Horse racing in Happy Valley happens every Wednesday! (except during summer). The races actually happen during evening-night time, making it one of the best attractions to do after a hard day of work.

And what is my favorite thing about it? You can drink beer and bet!

Betting actually starts from a very small amount (20 HKD), so usually we would set a small budget (200 HKD) and slowly spend it throughout the night. I don’t really encourage betting - we were doing it for the experience hahahah (and I never won).

What I really loved though was the atmosphere there.

You see the older generation of Hong Kong standing around with newspapers, studying numbers, doing calculations, discussing horses like their life depends on it. It makes you realize how deeply horse racing is connected to the culture of the city.

The environment becomes really thrilling once the race is about to start. People slowly gather near the track. Everyone is waiting. Then suddenly the horses fly past you at full speed. You really start to admire those animals.

That was probably the craziest part for me.

You can stand right next to the track and literally feel the wind from the horses when they pass by. The ground shakes a little, people start screaming, and for a few seconds the entire place feels electric.

I posted pictures here of us having the time of our lives at the racing track. Race night is really one of my favorite nights in Hong Kong.

For drinking, there are bars inside where you can grab beer. Or “DON’T TELL ANYONE” just buy beers from 7-Eleven hide them in your work bag.

There are multiple races throughout the night so the flow is basically:

Bet → Race → Break → Repeat.

The entrance uses the Octopus card actually (makes you realize how deeply it’s baked into Hong Kong culture).

You can come anytime and leave anytime during the event.

  • address: 跑馬地馬場
  • How to get there: You can take the old tram right to Happy Valley, then it’s a short 5-minute walk to the main entrance.

For more information you can look at the Happy Valley official site.

What was your experience going to horse racing? What do you think about the event? Did you manage to win anything (we only lose so far! hahah). Are there any other activities in Hong Kong that I missed out on / you want me to cover?
